data.title: Lam forecasts June quarter EPS $1.65 ± $0.15 as it raises 2026 WFE view to $140B with upside bias
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Lam Research (LRCX) Q3 2026

Management View

  • "Lam is off to a solid start in calendar year 2026 with revenues and profitability in the March quarter at the upper end of our guidance ranges and earnings per share exceeding the top end of our guided range." (President, CEO & Director Timothy Archer)

  • "We now expect WFE of $140 billion with a bias to the upside as the industry continues to work through various constraints." (President, CEO & Director Archer)

  • "In 2026, we see Lam's served available market, or SAM, percent of WFE expanding to slightly more than the mid-30s percent level, well on track toward our stated goal of high 30s percent over the next few years." (President, CEO & Director Archer)

  • "We now anticipate that this conversion will be pulled forward with the majority of spending occurring before the end of calendar year 2027." (President, CEO & Director Archer)

  • "Lam's advanced packaging revenue growth is expected to exceed 50% in calendar year 2026." (President, CEO & Director Archer)

  • "March quarter revenue came in at $5.84 billion." (Executive VP & CFO Douglas Bettinger)

  • "Customer Support Business Group generated a record $2.1 billion (sic) [ $2.11 billion ] in revenue in the March quarter." (Executive VP & CFO Bettinger)

Outlook

  • "We're expecting revenue of $6.6 billion, plus or minus $400 million." (Executive VP & CFO Bettinger) "Gross margin of 50.5%, plus or minus 1 percentage point." (Executive VP & CFO Bettinger) "We're forecasting operating margins of 36.5% plus or minus 1 percentage point." (Executive VP & CFO Bettinger) "We're forecasting record earnings per share of $1.65, plus or minus $0.15." (Executive VP & CFO Bettinger)

  • "I would encourage you to kind of keep it roughly in the levels that we just guided you to in June." (Executive VP & CFO Bettinger)

  • "We now expect WFE of $140 billion with a bias to the upside" (President, CEO & Director Archer), compared with the prior quarter call: "Our initial 2026 view is for WFE to be in the $135 billion range." (President, CEO & Director Archer)

Financial Results

  • "March quarter revenue came in at $5.84 billion, which was up 9% sequentially and up 24% from the same period in 2025." (Executive VP & CFO Bettinger)

  • "Gross margin in the March quarter was 49.9%." (Executive VP & CFO Bettinger) "March quarter operating margin was 35%." (Executive VP & CFO Bettinger)

  • "The March quarter diluted earnings per share came in at a record of $1.47." (Executive VP & CFO Bettinger)

  • "For capital return in the March quarter, we allocated approximately $800 million to share buybacks" (Executive VP & CFO Bettinger) and "we also retired $750 million of unsecured notes that reached maturity using cash from the balance sheet." (Executive VP & CFO Bettinger)

  • "Cash and cash equivalents totaled approximately $4.8 billion at the end of the March quarter." (Executive VP & CFO Bettinger) "Days sales outstanding was 64 days" (Executive VP & CFO Bettinger) and "inventory turns improved to 2.9x." (Executive VP & CFO Bettinger)

Q&A

  • Timothy Arcuri, UBS: Asked how gross margin reached the "50.5%" level so quickly and how to think about durability; Executive VP & CFO Bettinger: "keep it roughly in the levels that we just guided you to in June." President, CEO & Director Archer tied margin benefits to reliability: "That also yields benefits for us in terms of installation and warranty spending, which flows through to gross margin."

  • Christopher Muse, Cantor Fitzgerald: Asked about '27 visibility/slotting and CSBG sustainability; President, CEO & Director Archer: "we're having conversations with customers about '27" and "it feels like it's setting up to be a pretty good year in '27 right now." (Executive VP & CFO Bettinger) On CSBG, Executive VP & CFO Bettinger: "I think it's going to kind of sustain roughly at these levels as we go through the remaining quarters in the calendar year."

  • Harlan Sur, JPMorgan: Pressed on monetizing productivity improvements and on OpEx trajectory/target updates; President, CEO & Director Archer: "How do we monetize it? Yes, it's through services" and "in some cases, new tool sales." Executive VP & CFO Bettinger: "we need to give you an updated framework, and we will do that later in the year."

  • Atif Malik, Citi: Asked what changed in NAND and why down payments are at a low while WFE is expected to grow; President, CEO & Director Archer attributed NAND change to AI data center demand and conversions: "that's what's caused this acceleration." Executive VP & CFO Bettinger: "the group of customers that generally provide the down payments aren't the ones that are growing the quickest."

  • Stacy Rasgon, Bernstein: Challenged whether NAND conversion implies larger services/upgrades and whether WFE can step up further in 2027; Executive VP & CFO Bettinger: "it's not all just upgrades" and on next year, "clean rooms are going to be more available next year" and "WFE is going to be nicely growing next year." President, CEO & Director Archer added: "etch and deposition intensity rises" as clean rooms target advanced nodes.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Slightly negative analyst tone, focused on sustainability and constraints, including "how much capacity you have?" (Timothy Arcuri, UBS) and "what's wrong with that logic?" (Stacy Rasgon, Bernstein), with repeated probing on services durability, margins, China exposure, and the magnitude/timing of 2027 growth.

  • Slightly positive management tone, emphasizing durability, multiyear visibility, and execution, including "we remain confident in Lam's setup for continued outperformance." (Executive VP & CFO Bettinger) During Q&A, management stayed measured on specifics, for example: "we can't comment on any specific customer" (President, CEO & Director Archer) and "we're not going to give you a number" on lead times. (Executive VP & CFO Bettinger)

  • Compared with the prior quarter, management language appeared more assertive on the upcycle and forward visibility, moving from "clean room space constraints" (President, CEO & Director Archer, prior call) to "WFE of $140 billion with a bias to the upside" (President, CEO & Director Archer, current call), while analysts continued to test durability of margins and how constraints translate into 2027 upside.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Guidance and demand framing tightened upward: prior call "Our initial 2026 view is for WFE to be in the $135 billion range" (President, CEO & Director Archer) versus current call "We now expect WFE of $140 billion with a bias to the upside" (President, CEO & Director Archer).

  • Prepared remarks emphasis shifted toward specific share/portfolio wins and scaling the installed base opportunity, including: "we achieved both dielectric etch wins at a key foundry/logic manufacturer" (President, CEO & Director Archer) and "we delivered our first $2 billion-plus revenue quarter" in CSBG. (President, CEO & Director Archer)

  • Analyst focus stayed consistent on margins, services, China, and the clean-room constraint, but the current call added sharper questioning on the implied magnitude of NAND conversion/upgrade dollars and how that translates into Lam’s services growth.

Risks and Concerns

  • "We expect that China revenue in the June quarter will decline from these levels." (Executive VP & CFO Bettinger) Management linked lower down payments to mix: "down payments tend to come from smaller customers and a lot of them are in the China region." (Executive VP & CFO Bettinger)

  • Constraint risk remained central: "only so much that can be executed in this year" (President, CEO & Director Archer), with management describing the setup as constrained by "various constraints" even while maintaining an upside bias for WFE.

  • Profitability/opex scrutiny increased as spending rises: "We will be growing R&D investments throughout the remainder of the year" (Executive VP & CFO Bettinger) and "we also undertook a small workforce optimization focused on efficiency." (Executive VP & CFO Bettinger)

Final Takeaway

Management described record March-quarter revenue and a higher June outlook, alongside an upward revision in its 2026 WFE view to $140 billion with an upside bias. The call highlighted AI-driven inflections raising deposition and etch intensity, a pulled-forward NAND conversion timeline with most spending before the end of calendar 2027, and advanced packaging revenue growth expected to exceed 50% in calendar 2026. In Q&A, analysts repeatedly tested the durability of gross margins and the sustainability of services growth, while management pointed to operational efficiencies, tool reliability, and monetization via services and new tool sales, and reiterated plans to provide an updated long-term model later in the year.

data.title: Knight-Swift projects Q2 2026 adjusted EPS of $0.45 to $0.49 as truckload bid targets shift to high single to low double-digit increases
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Knight-Swift Transportation (KNX) Q1 2026

Management View

  • Adam Miller (CEO & Director) framed the quarter around tightening truckload conditions and regulatory-driven capacity exits, saying, "there are now more reasons to be optimistic about our industry than we have seen in over 4 years now" and adding that enforcement efforts "are in the early stages and are already having an impact on the market."
  • Miller said truckload pricing expectations changed materially versus the prior quarter, stating, "we have shifted our bid targets to a range of high single to low double-digit percentage increases on current pricing activity as compared to our low to mid-single-digit target 1 quarter ago."
  • Miller described a more disrupted bid environment, saying, "mini bid activity has increased" and "turnback bids are happening more frequently," while also noting that earlier peak-season outreach is appearing: "we have already had a number of shippers initiate discussions about peak season demand support, which is not typical this early in the year."
  • Andrew Hess (Chief Financial Officer) attributed the quarter’s loss to discrete items and weather/fuel disruption, stating, "GAAP earnings per diluted share for the first quarter of 2026 were a loss of $0.01" and "Adjusted EPS was $0.09 for the first quarter of 2026" while highlighting, "operating income declined by $38 million year-over-year."
  • Brad Stewart (Treasurer & Senior VP of Investor Relations) said Logistics faced both tightening capacity and higher qualification standards, noting, "Logistics revenue for the first quarter declined 9.9% year-over-year" and adding, "we further enhanced our already rigorous carrier qualification standards in response to a sharp increase in cargo thefts in the industry."

Outlook

  • Hess provided Q2 earnings guidance, saying, "we project our adjusted EPS for the second quarter of 2026 will be in the range of $0.45 to $0.49" and described the step-up as tied to nonrecurring Q1 disruptions and improving fundamentals.
  • Hess also flagged timing of contractual pricing flow-through, stating, "the recent strengthening of the truckload pricing environment will generally impact our contractual rates beginning late in the second quarter and into the third."
  • Compared with the prior quarter’s tone around bid season, management language moved from early-year rate expectations (Q4 call: "low to mid-single digits") to higher targets (Q1 call: "high single to low double-digit") and emphasized more immediate capacity security dynamics.

Financial Results

  • Hess reported consolidated performance drivers and unusual items, stating, "Consolidated revenue, excluding truckload and LTL fuel surcharge was essentially flat" and citing segment-specific headwinds including "the $18 million of expense for claim development in our LTL segment" and "an estimated $12 million to $14 million net negative impact for volume and cost headwinds from severe winter weather disruptions and sharply rising fuel prices."
  • Hess detailed truckload execution despite disruptions, stating, "our truckload adjusted operating ratio of 96.3% only degraded 70 basis points year-over-year" and adding, "Q1 marks the seventh consecutive quarter of year-over-year improvement in miles per tractor."
  • In LTL, Hess highlighted mix improvement and late-quarter momentum, saying, "Our LTL business grew revenue excluding fuel surcharge 2.6% year-over-year" and "March average daily tonnage up 7% year-over-year," while noting the year-over-year profitability impact from the claim development.
  • Stewart described Logistics operating dynamics under a tightening market, stating, "volumes were down 18.9%, while revenue per load grew 10.4%" and that gross margin was "16.6%" for the quarter.

Q&A

  • Christian Wetherbee, Wells Fargo Securities, LLC, Research Division: asked about truckload mid-cycle margin potential; Adam Miller (CEO & Director): "it's early in the inflection" but said regulatory pressure could pull more capacity, and added, "If we're able to do all 3 of those, then I do believe this sets up to be able to get back to a more normalized earnings or margin profile."
  • Richa Talwar, Deutsche Bank AG, Research Division: pressed on “normalized margins” and timing; Miller: "the Truckload business typically operates in the mid-80s" and said bid implementation timing means it "may not flow through to the P&L immediately," with momentum "more in the third quarter and then build into the fourth quarter."
  • Ken Hoexter, BofA Securities, Research Division: asked about proactive contract resets and LTL pricing; Miller: "we're getting mid-single digit on the renewals" and on truckload rate reviews said, "We're reviewing those that are, call it, the bottom 20% of performing."
  • Ravi Shanker, Morgan Stanley, Research Division: asked how regulatory catalysts evolve; Miller listed enforcement levers and said, "we've never seen this type of push to clean up some of the capacity"; Hess added, "the administration is committed to the cleanup."
  • Scott Group, Wolfe Research, LLC: asked about driver availability and shipper broker appetite; Miller said, "we're making investments" in recruiting and academies, and on shipper behavior stated, "I think they're going to have a bias towards asset-based carriers."
  • Jonathan Chappell, Evercore ISI Institutional Equities, Research Division: asked about LTL cadence and operating leverage; Miller said March was strongest and "those trends continuing into April"; Hess quantified momentum: "January, it was up 1.6%; February, 2.6%; and March, 6.9%."
  • Daniel Moore, Robert W. Baird & Co. Incorporated: asked about U.S. Xpress leverage and size; Miller said, "you're just under $2 billion between those 2, probably about $1.7 billion" (trucking and logistics), and Hess pointed to cost opportunities including "The CSA Crash BASIC... That's over 60% better than where we were at the acquisition."
  • Brian Ossenbeck, JPMorgan Chase & Co, Research Division: asked about Logistics carrier base cuts and hair testing; Miller said carriers are "down 30%" and Brad Stewart (Treasurer & Senior VP of Investor Relations): "the hair follicle test identifies roughly 14x the drug users that the urinalysis test does."
  • Ariel Rosa, Citigroup Inc., Research Division: asked about tractor reductions post-U.S. Xpress; Miller said, "we did change the standards -- the hiring standards" and emphasized, "we still have some empty trucks that we want to fill before we invest in additional capital."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was slightly positive but probing, repeatedly testing how durable the tightening is and how quickly it converts to margins (e.g., questions on "normalized margins" and bid timing).
  • Management tone was slightly positive and more confident than the prior quarter on pricing, with Miller emphasizing, "it certainly feels like the setup is there" while still using caution language such as, "it's early in the inflection."
  • Versus Q4, management language shifted from not "trying to call the inflection" to describing active tightening signals ("acute tightness," "elevated spot market") and materially higher bid targets.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Truckload pricing posture moved from Q4’s "low to mid-single" rate improvement discussions to Q1’s "high single to low double-digit" bid targets, alongside more emphasis on "turnback bids" and shippers acting "driven by a need to secure capacity."
  • The current quarter introduced discrete negative items and operational disruption (LTL claim development, Mexico VAT decision, winter weather/fuel impacts) that were not central in the prior quarter’s prepared narrative.
  • Analysts stayed focused across both quarters on bid season and margin restoration, but Q1 questions leaned more toward upside magnitude and timing as management described tightening as more tangible.

Risks and Concerns

  • Hess cited adverse items affecting results, including "the $18 million of expense for claim development" in LTL and "$4 million of expense" from a Mexico VAT reimbursement decision.
  • Stewart identified industry cargo theft and tighter carrier qualification standards as an operational headwind, stating, "this... resulted in a reduction in the number of existing carriers we are tendering loads to" and that it "caused us to reject more loads as unprofitable."
  • Miller flagged a potential second-half driver constraint, stating, "recruiting and retaining quality drivers have and will become more challenging" and that it could be an industry challenge "in the back half of the year."

Final Takeaway

Management described Q1 as disrupted by weather, fuel, and discrete claim/tax items, while emphasizing that truckload market conditions and bid activity tightened enough for Knight-Swift to raise bid targets to high single to low double-digit increases and guide to $0.45 to $0.49 in adjusted EPS for Q2. Executives repeatedly tied the tightening to regulatory-driven capacity reductions and said contractual pricing benefits should begin late in Q2 and build into Q3, while also pointing to improving LTL freight mix, ongoing U.S. Xpress margin gap closure efforts, and heightened Logistics carrier vetting amid rising cargo theft.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: IBM signals 5%+ 2026 revenue growth and about $1B higher free cash flow, while keeping guidance unchanged
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: International Business Machines (IBM) Q1 2026

Management view

  • “IBM is off to a strong start to 2026,” said CEO Arvind Krishna, pointing to “Revenue in the first quarter grew 6% and combined with strong margin expansion, drove 13% growth in free cash flow.”

  • Krishna said “Software revenue grew 8%, with Data and Red Hat growing double digits,” and added, “Infrastructure grew 12% with another record Z quarter, up 48%,” while “Consulting grew 1% with momentum in enterprise data and business application transformations.”

  • Krishna highlighted portfolio moves and partnerships, including “Confluent, which we closed this past quarter,” and said IBM “announced strategic collaborations with NVIDIA” and “a strategic collaboration with Arm to expand how AI workloads run across IBM infrastructure.”

  • Krishna introduced two product announcements: “IBM Bob, our AI-based software development system, is now generally available,” and “We also introduced Sovereign Core, software that lets organizations run AI workloads under their own operational authority within a defined jurisdiction and auditable controls.”

  • CFO James Kavanaugh said, “In the first quarter, we delivered 6% revenue growth, 140 basis points of operating pre-tax margin expansion, 17% adjusted EBITDA growth, 19% diluted operating earnings per share growth and $2.2 billion of free cash flow.”

Outlook

  • Kavanaugh reiterated full-year expectations: “The strong start to the year drives our confidence in delivering constant currency revenue growth of 5-plus percent in 2026 and free cash flow growth of about $1 billion year-over-year.”

  • Kavanaugh said IBM is holding its outlook despite Q1 momentum: “Given where we are in the year, we believe it is prudent to maintain our guidance even as the underlying performance and execution are off to an encouraging start.”

  • Segment direction was framed as: “Software business, which we now expect to grow 10-plus percent this year,” Consulting “to low to mid-single digits for the year,” and “we prudently continue to expect Infrastructure revenue to be down low single digits for the year, representing about a 0.5 point impact to IBM.”

  • For Q2, Kavanaugh guided, “we expect our constant currency revenue growth rate to be similar to the full year,” and “for operating pre-tax margin, we expect about 50 basis points of expansion as software mix and productivity are offset by dilution from the early closing of Confluent.”

Financial results

  • Kavanaugh said IBM’s “ARR was solid at $24.6 billion, up 10% since last year,” and attributed Data strength to “inorganic contribution from DataStax and Confluent, which closed in mid-March,” with “Data revenue grew 16%.”

  • Kavanaugh detailed profitability and cost actions: “Since 2023, this has driven $4.5 billion of productivity savings, with an additional $1 billion expected in 2026,” and said this quarter showed “expansion of operating gross profit margin by 110 basis points, adjusted EBITDA margin by 170 basis points and operating pre-tax margin by 140 basis points.”

  • On cash and balance sheet items, Kavanaugh said IBM ended Q1 with “cash of $11.8 billion,” “invested $10.5 billion in acquisitions,” “returned $1.6 billion to shareholders in the form of dividends,” and ended with “debt balance…$66.4 billion, including debt of $12.8 billion for our financing business.”

Q&A

  • Amit Daryanani, Evercore ISI: “how would you characterize your mix…infrastructure versus applications…consumption versus subscription…?,” Arvind Krishna: “4% of our portfolio…could be called an application,” and “our entire portfolio is very tied to consumption…capacity is equal to consumption.”

  • Wamsi Mohan, BofA: “how are you seeing the growth trajectory for the remainder of the software portfolio…?,” James Kavanaugh: “we exit first quarter feeling very confident,” and “we now see data up low 20-plus percent range…Red Hat…10% growth…Automation…record HashiCorp bookings.”

  • Wamsi Mohan, BofA: “is IBM's appetite for M&A changing…?,” Arvind Krishna: “the values that are out there right now are very attractive,” and “second half…maybe we can do something in the second half…[as] we are 100% sure that Confluent is off to a strong start.”

  • Benjamin Reitzes, Melius: “you guys rarely raise guidance…are you seeing something…?,” Arvind Krishna: “we are not seeing any of this slowing down,” while warning, “if the Strait stays closed…there could be energy impacts in Europe, but that is speculative.”

  • Fatima Boolani, Citi: asked for “quantitative framing” on AI inferencing workload mix, Arvind Krishna: “AI is adding a third kind of compute capacity,” and said “a fully populated system…can do about 450 billion inferences a day.”

  • Brent Thill, Jefferies: flagged software growth “downtick,” James Kavanaugh: “the entire 3-point drop quarter-to-quarter is…the mix of the portfolio,” adding, “in the fourth quarter…30%…transactional…[in] the first quarter…about 10%.”

  • Erik Woodring, Morgan Stanley: asked about “supply chain headwinds,” James Kavanaugh: “it has a de minimis impact to us overall,” and “we've been able to mitigate this dislocation overall.”

  • James Schneider, Goldman Sachs: asked about AI bookings and consulting risk, James Kavanaugh: said IBM is shifting to “outcome-based,” and cited “our AI platform…is north of $1.5 billion,” while Consulting had “30% of our backlog…GenAI now.”

  • Matthew Swanson, RBC: asked how IBM wins regardless of “GenAI application layer,” Arvind Krishna: “we were going to be neutral and Switzerland-like,” adding, “Whichever model you want to use, you can use. And wherever you want to run them, we'll help you run them.”

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly skeptical, pressing on guidance discipline and durability, including “Why not raise it?” (Benjamin Reitzes, Melius) and “What gives?” (Brent Thill, Jefferies).

  • Management tone was slightly positive but cautious on guidance, emphasizing execution while reiterating prudence: “we believe it is prudent to maintain our guidance” (CFO Kavanaugh) and “we are not seeing any of this slowing down” (CEO Krishna).

  • Versus last quarter’s more celebratory framing around full-year outperformance, this call leaned more to caution around uncertainty; Kavanaugh referenced “more uncertainty than there was 90 days ago,” while maintaining “we should be prudent.”

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Guidance posture was consistent with Q4, but Q1 explicitly connected guidance caution to uncertainty: Q1: “there's more uncertainty than there was 90 days ago,” versus Q4’s emphasis on entering 2026 “with momentum and confidence.”

  • Confluent shifted from pending to closed: Q4 described “our announced acquisition of Confluent,” while Q1 confirmed “Confluent, which we closed this past quarter,” alongside commentary on managing “dilution from the early closing of Confluent.”

  • AI metric disclosure changed as foreshadowed: Q4 said, “This will be the last quarter in which we report this metric separately,” and Q1 focused on AI embedded measures such as “about 30% of our backlog” in Consulting and software AI revenue “north of $1.5 billion.”

Risks and concerns

  • Macro and geopolitics were discussed as potential volatility: Krishna said “Uncertainties remain,” and added that a prolonged closure could mean “there could be energy impacts in Europe, but that is speculative.”

  • Supply chain and component costs were acknowledged: Kavanaugh said memory-related disruption has “a de minimis impact…overall,” but “will it impact storage…Absolutely,” with mitigation via “supplier optimization, supply chain diversification.”

Final takeaway

IBM’s management described Q1 as a “strong start to 2026,” driven by 6% revenue growth and $2.2 billion of free cash flow, and reiterated full-year targets of 5%+ constant-currency revenue growth and about $1 billion of free-cash-flow growth while “prudent[ly]” maintaining guidance. Executives emphasized software acceleration (including 10%+ full-year software growth expectations), ongoing mainframe AI monetization (including “450 billion inferences a day” capacity), and a “Switzerland-like” approach to deploying AI across models and infrastructure, while analysts focused questions on guidance conservatism, software durability, and operational risks like supply-chain costs.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Texas Instruments expects Q2 2026 revenue of $5B-$5.4B while targeting $2B-$3B 2026 CapEx
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Texas Instruments (TXN) Q1 2026

Management View

  • CEO Haviv Ilan opened by highlighting a major strategic move: "in the first quarter, we announced an agreement for TI to acquire Silicon Labs." He said, "We expect the transaction to close in the first half of 2027, subject to necessary approvals."
  • Ilan framed the quarter as part of an ongoing cycle turn, saying "the overall semiconductor market recovery is continuing" and adding that TI is "well positioned with inventory and capacity that allows us to support our customers with competitive lead times through the cycle." He attributed upside to end-market momentum: "revenue came in above the top of the range as we saw continued acceleration in industrial and data center."
  • CFO Rafael Lizardi summarized profitability and the quarter’s EPS item: "Net income in the quarter was $1.5 billion or $1.68 per share" and "Earnings per share included a $0.05 benefit for items not in our original guidance, primarily due to discrete tax benefits."
  • On capital returns and balance sheet, Lizardi said, "we paid $1.3 billion in dividends and repurchased $158 million of our stock" and "Our balance sheet remains strong with $5.1 billion of cash and short-term investments."

Outlook

  • Lizardi guided Q2 revenue to "the range of $5 billion to $5.4 billion" and EPS to "the range of $1.77 to $2.05," while adding, "We expect our effective tax rate to be about 13% in the second quarter."
  • Management reiterated caution on sustainability despite strong demand signals. Ilan said 2025 saw "a head fake, a false start" and emphasized, "We want to play quarter-by-quarter." Beckman added, "we just want to be mindful to just the overall macro backdrop and want to see how sustainable the growth is and that was factored in the guide."
  • On pricing, Ilan stated Q1 pricing was "stable, flat" and said, "I expect Q2 to be very similar." He also added conditionality for later in the year: "I think it's likely that prices may go up in the second half of the year," while stressing the "unknown for me right now is the sustainability of demand."

Financial Results

  • TI reported Q1 revenue of $4.8B and gross profit of $2.8B, with Lizardi stating gross margin was "58% of revenue" and "increased 210 basis points" sequentially.
  • Operating performance was described as operating profit of $1.8B ("37% of revenue"), with operating expenses of $974M.
  • Cash and investment intensity were framed around cash generation and inventory: Lizardi said cash flow from operations was $1.5B, Q1 capital expenditures were $676M, and inventory ended at $4.7B with "days...209, down 13 days sequentially."
  • On CHIPS-related inflows included in trailing results, Lizardi said trailing free cash flow included "$965 million of CHIPS Act incentives," including "a $555 million payment received in the first quarter" tied to Sherman, Texas production.

Q&A

  • Timothy Arcuri, UBS: asked if customer behavior reflects "rush orders" or "price increases" and whether June-quarter strength is mostly industrial; CEO Ilan: "Q1 was a continuation of what we saw in Q4" with growth "led by industrial" and supported by "the data center market," adding industrial growth "continued to accelerate through the quarter" and is what "guides our forecast into the second quarter."
  • Vivek Arya, BofA: questioned industrial growth drivers and checks against "double ordering"; CEO Ilan: "I don't have the evidence to show that," and said industrial demand was "broader" across "all sectors" and "all customers," adding industrial is "still 15% lower than the peak...back in 2022."
  • Joseph Moore, Morgan Stanley: probed fab loading, inventory trajectory, and incremental gross margin; CFO Lizardi: inventory is to "maintain high levels of customer service" and the expected fall-through is "in the 75% to 85%" range (excluding depreciation over the long term).
  • Stacy Rasgon, Bernstein: asked about Q2 gross margin drivers and acquisition accounting; CFO Lizardi: investors should factor "the acquisition charges line" continuing "every quarter until we close" and on reporting said, "we will do GAAP but we'll...give you all the pieces that you need to do your own non-GAAP."
  • Ross Seymore, Deutsche Bank: asked if pricing contributed to Q1 upside and whether memory costs are disrupting consumer markets; CEO Ilan: "pricing was stable" and "High level, we have not" seen evidence of memory-driven disruption.
  • Matthew Prisco, Cantor Fitzgerald: asked whether 2026 CapEx remains $2B-$3B and about depreciation and remaining CHIPS direct funding; CFO Lizardi: "yes" to "$2 billion to $3 billion of CapEx," "No change" to depreciation of "$2.2 billion to $2.4 billion," and remaining direct funding should come "over the coming years" as milestones are met.
  • Joseph Quatrochi, Wells Fargo: asked about free cash flow per share; CEO Ilan: "as long as revenue is growing mid- to high single digits, that $8 free cash flow per share is very probable" and added, "assuming we don't have another false start...we'll easily be at $8 free cash flow per share for 2026."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly skeptical, repeatedly testing sustainability (rush orders, double ordering, second-half deceleration, gross margin mechanics). Arya explicitly referenced prior-cycle deceleration, and Rasgon pressed on whether the Silicon Labs deal can be "accretive" under GAAP.
  • Management tone was slightly positive but guarded. Ilan paired optimism ("we are prepared" if the market accelerates) with caution ("We want to play quarter-by-quarter"), and Beckman repeatedly flagged the "macro backdrop" and sustainability.
  • Versus the prior quarter, management sounded more action-oriented on M&A and pricing optionality ("likely" second-half price increases) while keeping similar caution language about a "jittery" market and sustainability.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Strategic posture shifted from a market resegmentation discussion in Q4 (adding “data center” as a named end market) to Q1’s new M&A announcement: the planned acquisition of Silicon Labs with an expected close in H1 2027.
  • Guidance stepped up sequentially: Q4 guided Q1 revenue of $4.32B-$4.68B and Q1 guided Q2 revenue of $5B-$5.4B; Q1 messaging also introduced potential second-half pricing upside (“likely” if demand holds), whereas Q4 emphasized an assumption of low-single-digit price decline.
  • Analyst focus moved from Q1’s “above seasonal” setup and backlog duration (Q4 call) to Q2 sustainability, pricing resets, acquisition accounting, and the effect of acquisition charges (Q1 call).

Risks and Concerns

  • Management repeatedly highlighted uncertainty around demand durability. Ilan said the "unknown" is "the sustainability of demand" and referenced 2025’s "false start."
  • Automotive was described as steady but watch-listed for Q2, with Ilan saying, "I want to see automotive and see how it develops in Q2. It's too soon to call it," and noting China auto was down while "The rest of the world was up."
  • Supply-chain constraint risk was flagged around back-end capacity: Ilan said assembly and test is "a little bit of a tighter environment, at least externally," and management discussed redirecting investment to internal assembly/test.

Final Takeaway

Management described Q1 as demand broadening beyond a narrow recovery, led by industrial and data center, while guiding to higher Q2 revenue and EPS ranges and reiterating a disciplined capacity-and-inventory posture. Alongside the announced Silicon Labs acquisition expected to close in the first half of 2027, executives emphasized they are watching macro and demand sustainability closely—citing 2025’s “false start”—while noting pricing was stable in Q1 and could rise in the second half if demand remains strong.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Neptune outlines $195M 2026 revenue target and authorizes $100M buyback as Atlas+ beta expands
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Neptune Insurance Holdings Inc. (NP) Q1 2026

Management view

  • CEO & Chairman Trevor Burgess framed Neptune as “AI native” and said the company’s internal principle has been “No Humans,” explaining it was intended to build systems that work “faster, more consistently and at scale.” He said Neptune believes “the gap between Neptune and traditional insurance platforms is not narrowing it is widening,” and positioned AI as a way to expand the market, noting, “Tens of millions of properties in the United States remain uninsured for flood risk.”

  • Burgess highlighted post-quarter product release momentum, saying, “Following quarter end, we launched in a beta release, Atlas+, are a genetic assistant for insurance agents.” He added that early feedback included “examples of policies being sold directly as a result of these interactions,” and said the company expects Atlas+ “to become a core part of the sales workflow.”

  • President & Chief Risk Officer Matthew Duffy said the company rolled out “3 major technology advancements” tied to the platform’s access and build process: Atlas+ (agent conversational workflow), “our Neptune application inside ChatGPT,” and “Proteus, an internally developed AI software developer.” Duffy quantified internal development leverage: “In March alone, Proteus was responsible for over 30% of the engineering tickets completed.”

  • CFO James Steiner emphasized the asset-light MGA structure and operating leverage, stating, “we don’t carry any of the underwriting risk, the carriers do,” and “we scale by writing more code, not by hiring underwriters.”

  • Burgess announced capital return, saying, “today, we announced that our Board has approved a $100 million stock repurchase program,” and added, “We expect to fund this program through free cash flow over the next 2 years.”

Outlook

  • Burgess raised full-year expectations and said, “For the full year 2026, we now expect revenue of $195 million and an adjusted EBITDA margin between 60% and 61%.”

  • On the drivers of the raised revenue target, Burgess said the company is “quite bullish on the topline revenue trends,” adding, “This is not some hope and prayer that things get better later on. This is looking at the trajectory that’s here and now.”

  • On margins despite a lower Q1 seasonal profile, Burgess said management is “feeling quite good about the margin profile of the business,” and reiterated “60% being a floor rather than a ceiling to what this business can become.”

Financial results

  • Burgess reported Q1 results including “revenue of $37.8 million,” “net income of $7.3 million,” “adjusted EBITDA was $21.6 million,” and “written premium was $86.7 million,” and said “Premium in force reached approximately $389 million at quarter end.”

  • Steiner attributed profitability durability to timing effects and seasonality, stating, “The Q1 adjusted EBITDA margin was 57.1%, even though substantially, all of our public company accounting costs hit the P&L during the first quarter.” He added, “this is a timing dynamic, not a structural change in the business.”

  • On leverage and capital structure, Steiner said the company ended Q1 with “$227 million of total debt outstanding on the revolver, which is 2.2x trailing adjusted EBITDA,” and added, “Yesterday, we paid another $5 million down, bringing our current balance to $222 million.”

Q&A

  • Robert Cox, Goldman Sachs: Asked what Atlas+ is doing and how near-term impactful it is; CEO Burgess said Atlas+ is “a chat interface” that can “generate an e-mail script,” explain coverages, and “interact with the quote,” adding, “We are already seeing the impact of what is live today.”

  • Robert Cox, Goldman Sachs: Asked if higher revenue guidance is Q1-driven and whether margins trend to the low end; CEO Burgess said the guidance change reflects “the trajectory that’s here and now,” and on margins said Q1 is “the lowest” seasonally and that audit cost timing affected Q1, adding, “we’re feeling quite good about the margin profile.”

  • Joshua Shanker, BofA Securities: Asked if Milton/Helene comparison effects persist into Q2; CEO Burgess said, “I don’t think that’s an impact going forward.”

  • Joshua Shanker, BofA Securities: Asked about expansion beyond flood; CEO Burgess said, “flood remains our core focus,” but added, “we are running a beta test of earthquake,” and expects the beta to continue “for the next 3 or 4 months” before a California launch decision.

  • Charles Peters, Raymond James: Asked about new flood competitors; CEO Burgess said, “We are not seeing any meaningful impact from any competitor except for the NFIP,” citing NFIP at “approximately 85% of the business.”

  • Charles Peters, Raymond James: Asked about slow real estate as headwind and potential tailwind; President Duffy said a housing/refi pickup “would be a huge, huge tailwind to the business.”

  • David Motemaden, Evercore ISI: Asked about revenue retention ticking down; CEO Burgess tied it to pricing, saying last year renewals averaged “about 13%” increases, while this year is “positive mid- to high single digits.” Duffy added renewals are optimized for “lifetime value of the customer.”

  • David Motemaden, Evercore ISI: Asked about FEMA Advisory Council and NFIP exposure changes; CEO Burgess said, “we have no added information,” but added, “we are entirely prepared to flex and bring on as many customers as we need to.”

  • Thomas Mcjoynt-Griffith, KBW: Asked if low loss outcomes create pressure to reduce prices; CEO Burgess said Neptune will update lifetime loss ratio annually “at the end of the second quarter,” and described an opportunity for “higher volume in exchange for a slightly higher modeled loss ratio.”

  • Thomas Mcjoynt-Griffith, KBW: Asked economics of the ChatGPT app; CEO Burgess said, “no money is owed to any counterparty,” while noting binding is not inside ChatGPT and consumer behavior limits near-term impact.

  • Andrew Kligerman, TD Cowen: Followed up on FEMA process timing and ChatGPT binding; CEO Burgess said he had not heard of “specific legislation,” and added, “Currently, ChatGPT does not allow direct binding within the app itself. If they change that, we will be able to meet that immediately.”

  • Yaron Kinar, Mizuho: Asked whether guidance assumes a lighter hurricane season; CEO Burgess said $195 million “assumes that there is some storm activity but does not assume a very active storm season,” and quantified planning as “1.8 landfall hurricanes.”

  • Cave Montazeri, Deutsche Bank: Asked target leverage and buyback flexibility; CEO Burgess said, “We generally would like to stay below 2.5x,” and described potential opportunistic buybacks while using revolver capacity.

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was neutral to slightly skeptical, with repeated probing on retention drift, FEMA/NFIP uncertainty, competitive entries, and capital allocation (e.g., Evercore asked to “unpack some of the trends” behind retention; Deutsche Bank pressed on a “framework” for leverage).

  • Management’s tone was positive and confident, leaning on immediacy and preparedness, including “This is looking at the trajectory that’s here and now” (CEO & Chairman Trevor Burgess) and “we are entirely prepared to flex” on potential NFIP changes (CEO & Chairman Burgess).

  • Compared with Q4, management’s framing shifted from defending the stock’s AI narrative (“Neptune isn’t threatened by AI disruption. We are AI disruption.” in Q4) toward productized AI execution in Q1 (Atlas+, Proteus, ChatGPT app) and explicit shareholder returns via the newly approved repurchase program.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Guidance increased slightly quarter-over-quarter: Q1 updated full-year revenue to $195 million (vs. “approximately $193 million” in Q4), while maintaining the “60% to 61%” adjusted EBITDA margin range.

  • Capital return posture became more concrete in Q1 with the announcement that the Board approved a “$100 million stock repurchase program,” compared with Q4 commentary that buybacks were a potential lever alongside debt reduction.

  • Technology narrative evolved from broad AI positioning in Q4 to specific deployments and usage metrics in Q1, including Duffy’s statement that Proteus drove “over 30% of the engineering tickets completed” in March and the post-quarter Atlas+ beta rollout.

  • Analyst emphasis shifted from Q4’s heavier focus on AI disruption narrative and government shutdown effects to Q1’s focus on Atlas+ near-term impact, retention/pricing cadence, FEMA uncertainty, and leverage/buyback mechanics.

Risks and concerns

  • Burgess cited “the inherent variability of government policy and weather-related activity,” and in Q&A said the company had “no added information” on FEMA developments while preparing capacity to respond if the government “decides to reduce their exposure or get out of the business.”

  • Duffy flagged macro sensitivity to housing turnover, reiterating the “slow housing market” as a constraint on switching and new-shopping behavior, while pointing to a potential rebound as a sales catalyst.

  • On hurricane season assumptions and forecasting limits, Burgess said the revenue target assumes “some storm activity,” and highlighted uncertainty tied to Gulf conditions, describing the “Gulf temperature is extremely, extremely high.”

Final takeaway

Management described Q1 as a record first quarter with continued premium-in-force growth, maintained full-year profitability expectations at 60% to 61% adjusted EBITDA margins, and raised 2026 revenue expectations to $195 million. Alongside productizing its AI roadmap through Atlas+ and internal development automation (Proteus), Neptune paired its growth outlook with a newly authorized $100 million repurchase plan funded by free cash flow, while reiterating readiness for weather and potential NFIP-related policy shifts despite limited visibility from FEMA.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Cathay General projects 3.40%-3.50% 2026 NIM while ending rate-cut assumptions
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Cathay General Bancorp (CATY) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "We delivered solid financial performance in the first quarter, reporting net income of $86.9 million and diluted earnings per share of $1.29" (CEO, President & Director Chang Liu).

  • "We sold lower-yielding securities and reinvested at current market rates, a move that supports margin expansion and accelerates tangible book value recovery" (CEO Liu). He also cited capital actions: "we increased our quarterly cash dividend to $0.38 per share" and "our Board approved a new $150 million share repurchase program, subject to regulatory approval" (CEO Liu).

  • "Net interest margin of 3.43% grew 7 basis points compared to last quarter as deposit costs decreased" (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Albert Wang). He added on balance sheet positioning: "Period-end loans of $20.2 billion grew 0.2% linked quarter" and "Period-end deposits of $20.7 billion declined by 1% linked quarter" (Executive VP Wang).

  • "During the first week of April, we sold $210 million of lower-yielding mortgage-backed securities and reinvested $197 million into similar duration securities at significantly higher yields" (Executive VP Wang).

Outlook

  • "We continue to expect full year loan growth in the 3.5% to 4.5% range and deposit growth of 4% to 5%" (Executive VP Wang).

  • "Adjusted noninterest expense is still expected to increase between 3.5% to 4.5% for the year" (Executive VP Wang).

  • "Our NIM and NII outlook no longer assumes any rate cuts in 2026" (Executive VP Wang), while reiterating: "we remain confident in achieving our NIM target of 3.40% to 3.50%" (Executive VP Wang).

Financial Results

  • Diluted EPS of $1.29 vs. analysts’ $1.21 (current quarter analysts estimates).

  • "Our results reflected 2 noteworthy items that largely offset each other" (CEO Chang Liu): "a $17.3 million valuation gain on equity securities" and "a $15.7 million impairment on AFS debt securities from balance sheet repositioning" (CEO Liu).

  • "Net interest income of $194 million declined $0.8 million compared to last quarter due to day count, offset by margin expansion" (Executive VP Albert Wang).

  • "Noninterest expense decreased from $92.2 million to $86.7 million this quarter" (Executive VP Wang), driven by "$4.5 million of lower amortization expense on our low-income housing and alternative energy partnerships, along with lower compensation and benefit costs" (Executive VP Wang).

  • "Net charge-offs improved, dropping from $5.4 million last quarter to $2.1 million this quarter" and "our nonperforming asset ratio continued to trend in the right direction, improving from 59 to 51 basis points" (Executive VP Wang).

Q&A

  • David Chiaverini, Jefferies LLC: asked whether removing rate cuts from assumptions changes NIM trajectory; Executive VP Wang said, "without any cuts forecasted in, that's obviously going to put pressure and point us down slightly," but added the repositioning "should help by a few basis points for the year" and reiterated comfort with guidance.

  • David Chiaverini, Jefferies LLC: asked for repositioning yield details; Executive VP Wang said, "about 2.45% was the yield that we sold" and "the effective yield on that is like 5.33%" and estimated "probably about 2 to 3 basis points" of NIM benefit and "maybe $4 million" to net interest income.

  • Matthew Clark, Piper Sandler & Co.: asked about prepay/interest recoveries; Executive VP Wang said, "it was about $3.5 million this quarter," adding reported NIM "would have been 3.37%" without those items.

  • Matthew Clark, Piper Sandler & Co.: asked about low-income housing tax credit amortization run-rate; Executive VP Wang said it is "probably going to be in the $7 million to $8 million range for the next few quarters throughout the year."

  • Matthew Clark, Piper Sandler & Co.: asked why loan growth stayed cautious while maintaining the annual guide; CEO Liu pointed to "increased paydowns in our construction loan portfolio" and said, "our pipelines are still healthy and strong," with growth "more weighted towards the middle and the back end of the half."

  • Gary Tenner, D.A. Davidson: asked where 3.80% CDs rolled to; Executive VP Wang said first-quarter renewals were "around kind of the mid-3.50s" and described higher recent competitive pressure.

  • Gary Tenner, D.A. Davidson: asked about reserve build drivers and which portfolios were stressed; Executive VP Wang said the change was "a recalibration of one of the inputs" and added, "we look specifically at kind of the office portfolio" and "stress those portfolios a little bit more."

  • Andrew Terrell, Stephens: asked about expense run-rate and seasonality; Executive VP Wang said Q1 was "slightly lower on the comp and benefit" due to year-end incentives, and "the run rate is pretty good."

  • Andrew Terrell, Stephens: asked about proposed capital rules; Executive VP Wang said it "would be a huge win" and could mean "low kind of double-digit" risk-weighted asset reduction and "1.50% to 1.75%" boost to capital ratios.

  • Andrew Terrell, Stephens: asked about M&A; CEO Liu said the bank will look "more opportunistically" but "it's not the top priority at this point."

  • Kelly Motta, KBW: asked about fee income durability and wealth; CEO Liu said fee strength is "really the sort of the wealth business," and Executive VP Wang added, "we do have some new leadership in wealth" and "we're optimistic that wealth is going to hold in there."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst sentiment was neutral-to-slightly negative, emphasizing pressure points (rate-cut assumption change, deposit competition, loan growth softness, allowance build, fee durability).

  • Management sentiment was slightly positive in prepared remarks and pragmatic in Q&A, balancing confidence on targets ("we remain confident") with concessions on pressures ("that's obviously going to put pressure").

  • Versus last quarter, management messaging shifted from forecasting cuts (Q4: "we project 2 rate cuts in 2026") to removing them (Q1: "no longer assumes any rate cuts in 2026"), while reiterating the same NIM range.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Capital return messaging intensified: Q1 said it "completed" the prior $150 million buyback and added a "new $150 million" authorization (subject to regulatory approval), versus Q4 discussing "$12 million remaining" and intent to announce a new program after approvals.

  • Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer changed from Heng Chen (Q4) to Albert Wang (Q1).

  • NIM framing evolved: Q4 tied the 3.4%-3.5% NIM outlook to "2 rate cuts in 2026" (Executive VP Heng Chen), while Q1 reiterated the same target after removing cuts and highlighted a securities repositioning to support it (Executive VP Albert Wang).

  • Analyst focus stayed concentrated on margin drivers and deposit costs, while Q1 added deeper probing on securities repositioning economics and proposed capital rules.

Risks and Concerns

  • "Loan growth was softer than we anticipated" (CEO Chang Liu), with management attributing softness to construction paydowns and competitive refinancing.

  • Deposit competition was flagged: Executive VP Wang cited broker CD rates moving from "3.60% to 3.70%" earlier in the year to "4.00% to 4.05%" more recently.

  • Credit provisioning reflected macro caution: Executive VP Wang said the allowance increase was driven by "model updates, including a slight softening in the macroeconomic outlook," and the bank "stress[ed]" coastal office exposure.

Final Takeaway

Management described Q1 as a margin-expansion quarter supported by deposit cost actions and an AFS securities repositioning, alongside stepped-up capital return via a higher dividend and a new $150 million repurchase authorization (subject to regulatory approval). While management acknowledged softer-than-expected loan growth and heightened deposit rate pressure, it reiterated full-year targets for 3.5%-4.5% loan growth, 4%-5% deposit growth, and a 3.40%-3.50% net interest margin—now explicitly without assuming 2026 rate cuts.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Kinder Morgan tops Q1 estimates as gas demand surge offsets lower fuel volumes
data.description:
Kinder Morgan (KMI) reported first-quarter earnings above Wall Street expectations on Wednesday, helped by stronger natural gas demand tied to geopolitical tensions in the Middle East and rising power needs from data centers.

Shares rose 1% in extended trading; they've risen about 20% in the 12 months through today's close.

Pipeline operators in the United States have benefited from steady oil and gas production in the Permian Basin, while uncertainty surrounding shipping routes through the Strait of Hormuz has increased demand for U.S.-sourced liquefied natural gas.

“The geopolitical landscape became even more turbulent this quarter, with conflict in the Middle East joining the ongoing war in Ukraine as a source of significant commodity price volatility,” Executive Chairman Richard Kinder said in a statement.

The company said disruptions affecting competing LNG suppliers could also improve the longer-term outlook for U.S. gas consumption. Kinder Morgan (KMI) projects total U.S. natural gas demand will reach 150 billion cubic feet per day by 2031, up roughly 27% from current levels.

Kinder Morgan (KMI), one of North America’s largest energy infrastructure companies, also said it has agreed to buy Monument Pipeline, a 225-mile natural gas pipeline network serving Houston and surrounding markets, for $505 million in cash. The deal is expected to close in the second quarter of 2026.

Looking ahead, the company forecast 2026 net income attributable to Kinder Morgan to remain steady at $3.1 billion, while adjusted earnings per share are expected to increase 5% to $1.36. It also reiterated expectations for adjusted earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization of $8.6 billion this year.

For the quarter ended March 31, Kinder Morgan (KMI) posted adjusted earnings of $0.48 a share, topping analysts’ consensus estimate of $0.39.

Revenue rose to $4.83 billion, ahead of expectations for $4.55 billion.

Kinder Morgan (KMI) said it moved about 49,475 billion British thermal units of natural gas a day during the quarter, up from 45,978 billion Btu a day a year earlier.

Total products volumes, which include refined fuels such as jet fuel and diesel, declined to 1,965 thousand barrels a day from 2,047 thousand barrels a day in the prior-year period.

data.title: Everforth transition continues as management forecasts Q2 revenue of $970M-$1B and adjusted EBITDA margin of 8.8%-9.5%
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: ASGN Incorporated (ASGN) Q1 2026

Management View

  • “This will be our final earnings call under the ASGN name. And on Friday, we will officially begin operating as Everforth and trading under our new stock ticker, EFOR,” said (CEO & Director Theodore Hanson), adding the rebrand is intended to support “a more integrated operating model focused on higher-value solutions and deeper client relationships” and to “increase our cross-selling opportunities.”

  • Hanson said the company is changing commercial segment reporting “by industry rather than mode of delivery” and will disclose “our commercial consulting book-to-bill,” describing the change as aligned with its “Next Wave Growth Strategy and industry-led approach.”

  • On Q1 performance, Hanson highlighted “Revenues for the first quarter were $968.3 million” and pointed to Commercial demand in “AI and data, cloud and infrastructure and application engineering and modernization,” while noting “adjusted EBITDA margin of 8.6% was below our expectations for the quarter,” driven “largely by business mix.”

  • On Federal, Hanson reported “Federal segment new contract awards totaled $151.3 million” and said “Federal contract backlog was approximately $2.8 billion at quarter end,” while flagging that the company “experienced some funding delays at the Department of Homeland Security, which is navigating both a shutdown and a leadership transition.”

  • Hanson also tied near-term execution to leadership and M&A actions: “We welcome Ashish Jandial as President of Commercial North America; Sanjita Singh as President of India and International; and Donnie Scott as President of our Federal Government segment,” and “We also successfully closed the acquisition of Quinnox.”

  • “For the first quarter, revenues totaled $968.3 million,” said (Executive VP & CFO Marie Perry), adding “Quinnox contributed less than 1 month to the quarterly results,” while “SG&A expenses included $12.8 million in acquisition, integration and strategic planning expenses that were not included in our previously announced guidance estimates.”

Outlook

  • “For the second quarter of 2026, we are estimating revenues of $970 million to $1 billion, net income of $8 million to $13.7 million, adjusted EBITDA of $85 million to $95 million and adjusted EBITDA margin of 8.8% to 9.5%,” said (CFO Perry), with the guidance assuming “no further deterioration in the markets that we serve.”

  • Perry said Q2 includes “$8 million to $10 million in strategic planning expenses related to the execution of our Next Wave Growth Strategy, which we expect will decline over the coming quarters,” alongside “targeted initiatives that will generate meaningful structural cost savings for the business.”

  • On commercial demand and conversion dynamics into Q2, (President Sadasivam Iyer) said “sales cycles are getting slightly longer as in clients are deliberating longer before they pull the trigger on projects,” and added “some of that has been factored into the guide for Q2.”

Financial Results

  • Perry reported Q1 revenue of $968.3 million, including Commercial segment revenue of $675.5 million and Federal Government segment revenue of $292.8 million.

  • Margin and profitability items disclosed included gross margin of 27.5%, Commercial segment gross margin of 31%, Federal segment gross margin of 19.6%, net income of $5.5 million, adjusted EBITDA of $83.6 million, and adjusted EBITDA margin of 8.6%.

  • Perry said the margin pressure included mix plus “headwinds from changes in our foreign exchange rate related to our delivery center in Mexico,” and noted the quarter’s effective tax rate was 48.1% “reflecting the onetime discrete items not included in our guidance.”

  • Capital allocation and leverage items included the Quinnox purchase price of $290 million, share repurchases of $39 million for 0.8 million shares at an average price of $47.69, $934 million remaining under the $1 billion authorization, cash and cash equivalents of $143.6 million, and a net leverage ratio of 3.1x, with Perry stating: “We are committed to reducing our debt over time in order to bring our net leverage ratio closer to 2.5x target.”

  • Perry reported free cash flow of $9.1 million and said it was pressured “primarily due to an increase in DSO,” while (CEO Hanson) added: “No change in behavior, no increase in bad debt.”

Q&A

  • Jeffrey Silber, BMO Capital Markets: asked about the “lower-than-expected contribution from some higher-margin commercial solutions” and why visibility broke down; (CEO Hanson) said “the ramp-up of higher-margin solutions, especially in our enterprise software areas was slower and later into the quarter,” adding “it’s a gross margin issue. It’s not an expense issue.”

  • Jeffrey Silber, BMO Capital Markets: pressed on whether enterprise-software softness is “structural”; (CEO Hanson) cited “record bookings” exiting Q4 in “Workday… ServiceNow and Salesforce” but said “we just didn’t see the conversion to revenue at historical rates,” calling it “temporary” and pointing to Q2 margin guidance “solely on the back of improving gross margins.”

  • Margaret Nolan, William Blair: asked about financial services decline implications; (President Iyer) said big banks showed “continued tight management of expenditure,” while “we are seeing some green shoots in insurance and also some green shoots in diversified financials.”

  • Margaret Nolan, William Blair: asked about the 1.1x commercial consulting book-to-bill and deal duration; (President Iyer) said strength was “relatively broad-based,” with “longer-term bookings in cybersecurity” and “overall durations are shifting rightward and lengthening because of some of the cloud and infrastructure work.”

  • Kevin McVeigh, UBS: asked about the $12.8 million unanticipated expenses and Quinnox contribution; (CFO Perry) said the costs relate to implementing the multi-year savings effort and included “Quinnox… our go-to-market, our back office outsourcing and then our ERP,” and on Quinnox’s model she reiterated “$100 million” revenue contribution framework with “EBITDA margin of low 20%.”

  • Tobey Sommer, Truist: asked about assignment trends; (CEO Hanson) said Q4-to-Q1 was “down kind of mid-single digits, low to mid-single digits… seasonally about what we see every year,” with “Pay-to-bill margins pretty steady.”

  • Tobey Sommer, Truist: asked about federal budget implications; (CEO Hanson) said DHS is a “watch item,” but emphasized defense funding and that “AI… Data… Cybersecurity” should be key areas, adding “we’re expecting… a better second quarter than first quarter for sure.”

  • Jason Haas, Wells Fargo: asked about staffing “green shoots” and whether slowed high-margin work “push[ed] into 2Q”; (CEO Hanson) said IT staffing “really tracks IT spending,” while (President Iyer) said the issue was slower conversion and “some of that has been factored into the guide for Q2.”

  • Mark Marcon, Baird: asked about pricing pressure and DSO; (President Iyer) said “we’re not seeing a material compression in pricing,” describing the margin issue as “really timing,” while (CFO Perry) said free cash flow conversion is still “a good rule of thumb… 60% of our adjusted EBITDA converting.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative to pressing around margin drivers, enterprise platform conversion, and cash flow timing, including Silber’s challenge on structural risk and Marcon’s pricing-pressure questions.

  • Management tone in prepared remarks was slightly positive on strategic actions (rebrand, leadership, Quinnox) but more defensive/explanatory in Q&A on margins and conversion timing, with Hanson emphasizing “It’s a gross margin issue. It’s not an expense issue.”

  • Compared with the prior quarter’s more upbeat framing (record bookings and stronger margins), the current call’s tone shifted toward explaining execution timing and mix, while maintaining confidence via Q2 margin guidance and commentary that the softness was “temporary.”

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Strategic execution advanced from “soon to be Everforth” (prior call) to a definitive go-live: “on Friday… operating as Everforth and trading under… EFOR,” and the Quinnox deal moved from “intent to acquire” to “successfully closed.”

  • Profitability discussion shifted from Q4’s “Adjusted EBITDA margin was 11%” to Q1’s “adjusted EBITDA margin of 8.6% was below our expectations,” with management attributing the change to mix and timing rather than pricing.

  • Commercial bookings commentary cooled from Q4’s “record $444.4 million… book-to-bill of 1.3x” to Q1’s trailing 12-month commercial consulting book-to-bill of 1.1x, and Q&A concentrated on conversion from enterprise-platform bookings into revenue.

  • Federal narrative moved from shutdown-delayed awards and “pent-up demand in Q1” (prior call) to “award activity… pick up” post-budget passage, while introducing specific DHS funding delays tied to “a shutdown and a leadership transition.”

Risks and Concerns

  • Hanson cited macro uncertainty: clients face “a very volatile macro environment with continued uncertainty around how technologies such as AI and enterprise software will ultimately impact the technology landscape and influence their IT spending,” which management linked to “near-term variability.”

  • Federal risk was framed around DHS: “funding delays at the Department of Homeland Security,” with management positioning defense, intelligence, and national security as better-funded areas.

  • Margin risk was tied to mix and conversion timing in higher-margin enterprise platforms; management’s mitigation was “disciplined expense management,” structural cost savings initiatives, and guidance that embeds a higher Q2 margin range.

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q1 as a transition quarter into the Everforth brand and operating model, while acknowledging a margin shortfall tied to slower enterprise-platform revenue conversion and a less favorable mix. Leadership additions and the closed Quinnox acquisition were positioned as building blocks for higher-value solutions and offshore delivery scale, while Q2 guidance called for $970 million to $1 billion of revenue and an adjusted EBITDA margin of 8.8% to 9.5%, supported by improving gross margin, ongoing cost actions, and moderating strategic-planning expenses.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Booz Allen sinks as Bessent says Treasury has lost confidence in the company for government contracts
data.description:
Booz Allen Hamilton (BAH) closed down 4.5% on Wednesday after Treasury Secretary Bessent told a U.S. Senate hearing that the government contractor had lost his agency’s confidence.

Bessent said the loss of confidence in Booz Allen (BAH) led to the agency’s decision to cancel its contracts with the consulting firm earlier this year, in a surprise move that came years after a former employee leaked tax returns to media outlets.

"We no longer have confidence in that firm’s ability to screen, vet, and deploy contractors within the IRS or indeed the entire Treasury Department," Bessent said during a Senate Appropriations Financial Services and General Government Subcommittee hearing as he explained the agency's decision to cancel its contracts with the company.

When the Treasury Department canceled ~$21M in contracts, it said the decision stemmed from deficiencies in data security controls tied to Booz Allen's (BAH) work with the Internal Revenue Service.

Booz Allen (BAH) shares have dropped 24% since the cancellation.

data.title: Century Communities outlines 2026 deliveries of 9,500 to 10,500 amid reduced guidance
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Century Communities (CCS) Q1 2026

Management view

  • Executive chairman Dale Francescon said macro conditions “intensified even further beginning in early March,” citing “geopolitical issues and increased economic uncertainties, coupled with higher interest rates and gas prices” that “weighed on our order activity most meaningfully in March.” He highlighted balance-sheet and operational actions, including that the company “repurchased approximately 2% of our shares outstanding at the beginning of the year at a 27% discount to our book value” and “increased our quarterly cash dividend by 10% to $0.32 per share.”

  • CEO Robert Francescon described a strategy shift from Q4 to Q1: “While in the fourth quarter of last year, we focused more on pace versus price, we took the more balanced approach in the first quarter 2026 that we outlined on our conference call last quarter.” He said March demand softened year-over-year and the quarter ended with “net new orders totaling 2,379 homes,” while “our cancellation rate of 12.2% in the first quarter was below the levels we experienced throughout most of 2025.”

  • Chief Financial Officer John Dixon reported profitability and capital returns, stating, “In the first quarter, pretax income was $33 million and net income was $24 million or $0.84 per diluted share,” adding, “Adjusted net income was $26 million or $0.88 per diluted share.”

Outlook

  • CFO John Dixon reduced full-year targets, saying, “We are reducing our full year 2026 home delivery guidance by 5% and now expect it to be in the range of 9,500 to 10,500 homes, and our home sales revenues to be in the range of $3.5 billion to $3.8 billion.”

  • CEO Robert Francescon framed near-term sales conditions as volatile but improving from March, stating, “Our order activity so far in April has trended better than March with orders also improving sequentially over the past several weeks.”

  • On incentives and margins into Q2, CEO Francescon said, “Assuming current market conditions, we expect incentives on closed homes in the second quarter of 2026 to be similar with first quarter levels,” while CFO Dixon added that in Q2 “the most significant driver of our adjusted homebuilding gross margin [will] continue to be incentives needed to generate an acceptable sales pace.”

Financial results

  • CFO John Dixon said, “Home sales revenues for the first quarter were $734 million with our average sales price of $365,000,” and noted total deliveries of “2,013 homes during the first quarter.”

  • On profitability drivers, CFO Dixon said, “Our first quarter 2026 GAAP homebuilding gross margin of 17.8% increased by 240 basis points over fourth quarter 2025 margins of 15.4%,” and added, “Our adjusted gross margin in the first quarter was 19.7% compared to 18.3% in the fourth quarter of 2025.” He also disclosed a one-time margin benefit: “Our first quarter margin benefited by 90 basis points from a reduction to our warranty accrual and rebate collections in excess of previous estimates.”

  • CFO Dixon described cost and overhead dynamics: “SG&A as a percentage of home sales revenue was 15.8% in the first quarter and impacted by lower-than-expected deliveries,” and said the company expects “SG&A as a percentage of home sales revenue of 14.5% for the second quarter.”

  • CFO Dixon highlighted segment contribution and balance sheet, saying, “Revenues from financial services were $22 million in the first quarter, and the business generated pretax income of $8 million,” and added, “We ended the quarter with $2.6 billion in stockholders' equity and $886 million of liquidity.”

Q&A

  • Alexander Rygiel, Texas Capital Securities: asked about competitors’ spec inventory adjustments and broad spec levels; CFO Dixon replied, “we're comfortable with most markets with where the overall finished spec inventory is at,” adding the focus is “to really ensure at a community level” the company is strong on “pricing as well as consumer demand.”

  • Alexander Rygiel, Texas Capital Securities: asked when geographic expansion could reaccelerate; CEO Robert Francescon said, “We're now in over 45 markets coast to coast,” and added, “our biggest focus is growing within our existing footprint,” targeting “a top 10… or… top 5 position” in markets.

  • Natalie Kulasekere, Zelman & Associates: asked about vendor fuel surcharges and cost inflation risk; CEO Francescon said, “to date, we've been able to avoid price increases,” adding, “Is that something that's going to be a topic in Q3 and Q4? Don't know.”

  • Natalie Kulasekere, Zelman & Associates: asked about land sale detail and pipeline; CEO Francescon said it was “just an opportunistic item” involving “back half lots that we did not need for the foreseeable future.”

  • Jay McCanless, Citizens JMP: asked which regions were more affected and what April shows; CEO Francescon said, “the Southeast still remains really strong,” cited “green shoots” in Austin, and said “the Bay Area has probably been the slowest or the weakest market.”

  • Michael Rehaut, JPMorgan: asked how April is trending and whether incentives can stay flat; CEO Francescon said April is “better than March,” and on incentives, “it’s always fluid,” but “we feel fairly comfortable… we will be flat at worst from where we were in Q1 to where we'll be in Q2.”

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was neutral to slightly positive, with multiple questions probing whether macro uncertainty is driving deeper incentive pressure and whether regional softness (notably the Bay Area) could worsen.

  • Management’s tone was slightly cautious but steady, repeatedly tying weaker March demand to external shocks; CEO Robert Francescon emphasized near-term improvement with “April has actually started out better than March,” while also acknowledging uncertainty with “it’s always fluid.”

  • Versus the prior quarter, management language shifted from optimism around a “balanced approach” entering 2026 to more explicit macro-driven caution and a guidance reduction tied to “the conflict in the Middle East” and “higher interest rates and gas prices.”

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Guidance direction changed: in Q4 2025, CFO Dixon guided 2026 deliveries of “10,000 to 11,000 homes” and revenue of “$3.6 billion to $4.1 billion,” while in Q1 2026 he lowered them to “9,500 to 10,500 homes” and “$3.5 billion to $3.8 billion.”

  • Incentives commentary remained central, but the near-term trajectory tightened; Q4 anticipated Q1 incentives “improve by up to 50 basis points,” while Q1 said Q2 incentives are expected to be “similar with first quarter levels.”

  • Capital return actions increased in Q1, with a dividend raise to $0.32 and $40 million of buybacks, versus Q4’s $0.29 dividend level and $20 million of buybacks.

Risks and concerns

  • Executive chairman Dale Francescon cited “geopolitical issues,” “increased economic uncertainties,” “higher interest rates and gas prices,” and said these factors “further eroded consumer settlement.”

  • Incentive dependence was framed as a margin risk; CFO John Dixon said the “most significant driver” of adjusted gross margin remains “incentives needed to generate an acceptable sales pace.”

  • Cost inflation risk from fuel was raised by analysts; CEO Robert Francescon said, “there's a lot of headlines on oil and petroleum products,” but added, “to date, we've been able to avoid price increases.”

Final takeaway

Management attributed a March demand step-down to geopolitical and macro uncertainty, then lowered full-year 2026 delivery and revenue guidance while maintaining a “balance pace and price” operating stance. The company emphasized inventory control, flexible land options, and shareholder returns (higher dividend and continued buybacks), while signaling that near-term profitability remains highly sensitive to incentive levels and interest-rate-driven affordability conditions.
